title: React Error Boundaries Quiz
description: Evaluate your understanding of catching errors, lifecycle methods, and fallback UI in React.

questions.question: Which and/or what is the primary role of an Error Boundary in React?
options: To speed up the app by caching components.
To catch JavaScript errors anywhere in their child component tree and display a fallback UI.
To replace the standard try-catch blocks in JS.
To prevent any error from ever occurring.

questions.question: Can a functional component be an Error Boundary in the latest stable version of React?
options: Yes, using the useError() hook.
No, only class components can be error boundaries currently.
Yes, but only in older versions of React.
None of the above.

questions.question: Which lifecycle method should you use inside an error boundary component to render a fallback UI?
options: static getDerivedStateFromError()
componentDidCatch()
componentDidMount()
renderError()

questions.question: Will an Error Boundary catch errors inside event handlers?
options: Yes, it catches all errors.
No, it does not catch errors inside event handlers (regular try-catch should be used there).
Only if you return them.
Only in older versions of React.

questions.question: What happens if an Error Boundary itself throws an error?
options: It will be automatically caught by itself.
It will bubble up to the next closest error boundary above it in the tree.
React logs a warning and continues.
The app crashes instantly.
